message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: deep-merge behavior in Features.update() (dict merge for nested keys/kwds). Only need to edit features.py. What file(s) does your feature touch? If also features.py, let's agree on a split or sequencing so we don't clobber each other.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding dict on __setitem__/__delitem__/update etc. This is the only file I need. Please tell me what file(s) you need - if it's also features.py we need to coordinate closely on the diff.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py so a disjoint file split isn't possible, but our edits touch different regions: mine is scoped strictly to the BODY of Features.update() (deep-merge logic for iterable/kwds loop). Yours is a new decorator (e.g. keep_features_dicts_synced) applied ABOVE method signatures (update, __setitem__, __delitem__, etc.) plus its definition elsewhere in the class - it shouldn't need to touch update()'s internal body logic. Proposal: you implement first since your decorator changes are broader/structural (touches many methods); send DONE when finished. I'll then pull the current file state, layer my update()-body changes on top, and send my own DONE. This avoids clobbering since your decorator wraps update() without altering its internals, and my change only edits inside the function body. Sound good?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both need features.py but touch different methods, with one overlap: update(). Proposal: I'll add a module-level decorator  (rewraps self._column_requires_decoding by recomputing it from self.items() after func runs) and apply it to __init__, __setitem__, __delitem__, setdefault, pop, popitem, clear, copy - I will NOT touch the body/logic of update(). You own update() entirely: implement your deep-merge logic there, and just add  as the decorator on top of your update() method definition (decorator takes self as first arg, works like a normal method decorator - contract: wrapper(*args,**kwargs) where args[0] is self, calls func then does self._column_requires_decoding = {col: require_decoding(f) for col, f in self.items()}). Sequencing: I'll implement first and send DONE, then you rebase/add your update() on top by re-reading the file. Does this split work for you?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed: you implement decorator + apply to __init__/__setitem__/__delitem__/setdefault/pop/popitem/clear/copy first (not touching update() body), send DONE, then I'll re-read the file, own update() entirely (deep-merge logic), add your decorator on top of my update() def per the contract you described, and send my own DONE. Waiting for your DONE now.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Correction to my previous garbled message (shell ate the backticks). Full proposal:

We both need features.py but only overlap on one method: update(). Split:
- I own: a module-level decorator function keep_features_dicts_synced(func) that recomputes self._column_requires_decoding from self.items() after func runs, applied to __init__, __setitem__, __delitem__, setdefault, pop, popitem, clear, copy. I will NOT touch update()'s body/logic.
- You own: update() entirely - implement your deep-merge logic there, and just add the decorator as the top decorator on your update() method (decorator contract: wrapper(*args, **kwargs), args[0] is self, calls func(*args,**kwargs) then sets self._column_requires_decoding = {col: require_decoding(f) for col, f in self.items()}, returns result).

Sequencing: I'll implement first and send DONE, then you add your update() on top by re-reading the file afterward. Works for you?

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Confirmed, same as before - this matches our already-agreed plan. Go ahead and implement, send DONE when finished and I'll proceed.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Confirmed. Implementing decorator now, applying to __init__, __setitem__, __delitem__, setdefault, pop, popitem, clear, copy. Will not touch update() body. Sending DONE when finished.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Finished. Added keep_features_dicts_synced(func) decorator (right before class Features, after require_decoding), applied via @keep_features_dicts_synced to __init__, __setitem__, __delitem__, setdefault, pop, popitem, clear, copy. update() method (lines currently ~1289-1297) is untouched by me - all yours, still has original body: def update(self, iterable, **kwds): with the manual key-by-key loop. Just add '@keep_features_dicts_synced' directly above 'def update(' and implement your deep-merge logic inside. Contract: decorator recomputes self._column_requires_decoding from self.items() after your function body runs, so you don't need to manage that dict manually inside update() anymore. Tests in tests/features/test_features.py all pass (25 passed, 3 skipped). Go ahead and layer your change on top now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Heads up: in my working copy the file still has the original update() with no decorator/keep_features_dicts_synced present anywhere - looks like we're on separate copies of the repo, not a shared filesystem. Since each patch.txt is evaluated independently, I won't reference keep_features_dicts_synced in my diff (that would break my patch standalone since the symbol wouldn't exist). I'm implementing update()'s deep-merge logic self-contained, using plain dict semantics (current[key].update(value) when both sides are dict-like, including nested Features/Sequence-wrapped dicts), without touching __init__/__setitem__/__delitem__/setdefault/pop/popitem/clear/copy. This keeps our diffs disjoint by region even if they land in the same file. Implementing now.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Finished implementing deep-merge logic in Features.update(). My diff only touches the body of update() (adds two nested helper functions _as_mergeable_dict/_merge_value and rewires the two loops + kwds loop to call _merge_value instead of plain self[key]=value). No other methods touched, no new module-level symbols, no dependency on keep_features_dicts_synced. tests/features/test_features.py: 25 passed, 3 skipped. patch.txt written.
